target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)
- [x] All 129 hooks-related unit tests pass locally (`server.hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts`, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts`)
- [x] Plugin contract shard `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-c` (498 tests including `extension-runtime-dependencies`) passes locally after the slack typebox fix
- [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against a live OpenClaw setup (see Real behavior proof section above)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

cc @steipete @vincentkoc — you own the touched files per blame. Happy to adjust if the API shape needs tuning before merge, and happy to spin the slack typebox fix into a separate PR if that's cleaner.

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the pinned startup route registry misses, the gateway should try a scoped manifest-planned route registry and handle the matching plugin route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the gateway/plugin-registry seam, not inside route matching or manifest parsing alone.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway plugin HTTP requests can now activate route-owning plugins from manifest `activation.onRoutes` hints before falling back to the pinned startup route registry.
- Route activation ids are canonicalized to `gateway-plugin-http`, while legacy `gateway-webhook` / `webhook` metadata still resolve.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss] -> [false]

After:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss]
                -> [manifest route-owner planning] -> [scoped route registry]
                -> [match] -> [handler]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: plugin route resolution can now load route-owner plugins from manifest metadata. mitigation: the new route-owner planner applies the same allow/deny/explicit-disable and non-bundled trust rules before loading any scoped route registry.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway plugin HTTP routes
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config plus synthetic test configs

### Steps

1. Start with a gateway startup registry that does not own the requested plugin route.
2. Plan route owners from manifest `activation.onRoutes` metadata.
3. Load the scoped route registry and re-run route matching.

### Expected

- Matching route-owner plugins are loaded through the scoped registry.
- Untrusted or disabled route owners stay excluded.
- Legacy route aliases still normalize to the canonical route id.

### Actual

- Verified locally with seam and parser/planner tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - route activation alias normalization (`webhook` / `gateway-webhook` -> `gateway-plugin-http`)
  - route-owner trust filtering for bundled/global/workspace plugins
  - gateway plugin HTTP fallback to scoped registry on startup-registry miss
- Edge cases checked:
  - non-empty allowlists still constrain route owners
  - explicit workspace disablement blocks route-owner loading
  - no planned owners means no scoped load
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full end-to-end gateway runtime with live channel/plugin traffic

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: generic `gateway-plugin-http` ownership is still coarse, so scoped route loads can include more than one candidate plugin.
  - Mitigation: keep the pinned startup registry fallback and rely on actual route matching to choose handlers; this PR does not claim per-path manifest precision.
- Risk: non-bundled route owners could become an unsafe auto-load path.
  - Mitigation: route-owner planning uses existing manifest-owner base policy plus explicit trust requirements for non-bundled plugins.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: fix: accept valid MCP tool schemas without strict inputSchema.type check
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes MCP server loading failure when tools have `inputSchema` without explicit `type: "object"`.

Closes #63602

## Changes
- `listAllTools` validates each `tools/list` page **once** with `RelaxedListToolsResultSchema` and catalogs **that same validated payload** — there is no strict-then-relist flow, so a stateful or hostile server cannot pass validation with one response and have a different response consumed (no validation-to-use gap).
- `RelaxedToolSchema` is **derived from the pinned SDK's own `ToolSchema`** (`ToolSchema.extend` / `ToolSchema.shape.inputSchema.extend`) and differs from it at exactly one point: the root `inputSchema.type` is an optional string instead of `z.literal("object")`. Every other field — `outputSchema`, task `execution` metadata, `annotations`, pagination — keeps the exact pinned SDK contract, so malformed metadata still rejects the page.
- After every page parse, the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` is invoked with the validated tools — the same side effect `client.listTools()` runs — so `callTool()` enforces output-schema validation and task-required guards identically to the SDK flow. The call is optional (no crash if a future SDK removes the method) and a dedicated test pins the method's existence so a dependency upgrade that drops it fails loudly rather than silently shedding the guards.
- `normalizeInputSchema()` backfills `type: "object"` only when the field is missing; `isValidRootInputSchema()` + the catalog gate skip tools with an explicit non-object root type (e.g. `"string"`, `"array"`) with a `logWarn`. Server `toolCount` reports accepted tools only.

## Root Cause
The MCP SDK v1.29.0 `ToolSchema` validates `inputSchema.type` as `z.literal("object")`. Some MCP servers (especially older SDKs or custom implementations) omit `type` from `inputSchema`, returning `{ properties: { ... } }`, which trips a Zod validation error: `invalid_value expected "object" in inputSchema.type` and crashes the whole catalog.

## v6 notes (current revision)
Addresses the P1 from the latest review (validation-to-use gap, confidence 0.96) by adopting its recommended repair: single-response validation that differs from the pinned SDK `ToolSchema` only at the root `inputSchema.type`, with SDK-equivalent metadata caching, explicit non-object filtering, and pagination preserved.
- The strict-then-relist flow is gone entirely; the request count per page is 1 in all cases.
- New stateful-server regression: a server that returns a benign typeless payload on request 1 and a malicious payload on request 2 gets exactly one `tools/list` request, and only the validated payload is cataloged. **Mutation-checked**: against the previous strict-then-relist implementation this test fails with the catalog containing the drifted (never-strictly-validated) tool — reproducing the reported attack — and the server log showing two list requests.
- New SDK-seam pin test: `Client.prototype.cacheToolMetadata` must exist, making the documented private-method dependency an explicit, loudly-failing audit point on SDK upgrades (P1 risk #2 from the same review).
- Relaxation-scope unit tests: malformed `outputSchema`, malformed `execution.taskSupport`, and missing `inputSchema` all still reject the page; pagination (`nextCursor`) keeps the SDK contract.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Bundle MCP tool cataloging accepts tools whose root `inputSchema` omits `type` by validating each `tools/list` response once against a schema derived from the pinned SDK contract (relaxed only at the root `inputSchema.type`), backfills `type: "object"`, skips explicit non-object roots, keeps servers with unrelated strict failures rejected, never issues a second list request, and preserves the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` side effects for every page.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS from the rebased PR head, Node 25.2.1, pnpm 11.2.2, driving the real bundle MCP runtime (`getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` → `getCatalog()` → `callTool()`) against four real out-of-process stdio MCP servers, one of them stateful (different payload per request).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm test src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on both files, plus a live four-server e2e script via `pnpm exec tsx`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  42 passed (42)
pnpm tsgo:core / tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint / oxfmt --check: clean

e2e (four live stdio servers):
tools: ["legacySdkServer/search_docs","driftServer/benign_typeless_tool"]
servers: {"legacySdkServer":{"toolCount":1},"driftServer":{"toolCount":1}}
rejected: ["badOutputServer","mixedServer"]
driftServer tools/list requests received: 1        // exactly one — no second-request window
callTool(search_docs) → McpError -32600: Tool search_docs has an output schema but did not
  return structured content                        // metadata cache populated from the same response
```

Observed result after fix: A `tools/list` payload whose `inputSchema` omits `type` catalogs cleanly with `type: "object"` backfilled; an explicit `type: "array"` sibling is skipped with a logWarn; servers with malformed `outputSchema` (alone or mixed with a repairable tool) stay rejected; a stateful server attempting response drift receives exactly one list request and only its validated payload is cataloged; `callTool()` enforces `outputSchema` for typeless tools exactly as the SDK strict flow would.

What was not tested: A live third-party MCP server connected to an actual model-provider tool call was not tested in this environment.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

standard tier output  →  1 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]

full     tier output  →  3 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/core/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/persona/SOUL.md  [hook]


===== BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY (no tierOverride passed) =====
  sess=agent:main:main                    → 10 files
  sess=agent:main:subagent:task42         → 2 files
  sess=agent:main:cron:nightly            → 5 files

Done.
```

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: doctor: add memory search lint findings
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds `core/doctor/memory-search` as a structured Doctor lint finding source.
- Reuses the existing memory-search doctor note logic through an injected note sink so legacy `openclaw doctor` output stays unchanged.
- Keeps the new structured check opt-in/default-off: it is available through `--only core/doctor/memory-search` or `--all`, but plain `doctor --lint` does not run it.
- Keeps lint read-only/safe: skips informational disabled-memory notes, treats unprobed key-optional readiness as skipped, suppresses configured QMD binary probing, and skips auth-profile/OAuth resolution from lint detection.

## Contract / Safety
- No plugin SDK/public contract changes (`plugin-sdk:surface:check` and `plugin-sdk:check-exports` clean).
- No repair behavior or data model change.
- Default `openclaw doctor --lint` behavior is unchanged for memory search because `core/doctor/memory-search` has `defaultEnabled: false`.
- `openclaw doctor --lint --only core/doctor/memory-search` reports config/auth findings from read-only config/env state only; it does not execute configured QMD commands or refresh/persist auth profiles.

## Proof
- Head: `b72593b0d3447b9c3eb708df99af83d29a124f0c` rebased onto `830467bc93065f6ef5a1302b3c86bf14c930c3df`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (2 shards, 105 tests)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-memory-search.ts src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/doctor-memory-search.ts src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`
- Source CLI proof with isolated config/state:
  - missing OpenAI key: `pnpm openclaw doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/memory-search` exits 1 with `core/doctor/memory-search` warning at `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider` and `OPENAI_API_KEY` fix hint.
  - QMD no-spawn: config points `memory.qmd.command` at a marker-writing script; `pnpm openclaw doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/memory-search` exits 0, returns no findings, and marker file is absent.
  - default-off: plain `pnpm openclaw doctor --lint --json` on the same warning config reports `memoryFindings: 0`; nonzero exit came from unrelated default checks in the isolated config.

## Not Tested
- Packaged installer/runtime proof; this is source CLI + focused unit/type/lint coverage for a default-off Doctor lint detector.

target.title: Add hosted catalog source profile validation
target.body: ## Summary  This PR restacks the hosted catalog source-profile slice on top of the merged persistent snapshot work in #95964.  It adds local source-profile validation for hosted catalog entries:  - resolves the default `clawhub-public` feed profile to `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins` - keeps direct `feedUrl` overrides constrained to the public hosted-feed allowlist - filters install candidates whose `sourceRef` is missing or unknown to local source profiles - requires manifest install source refs when a non-default or locally overridden feed profile is used - accepts trusted, available ClawHub feed entries with `sourceRef: public-clawhub` - normalizes ClawHub `sha256:<hex>` candidate integrity to SRI for the ClawHub install path without attaching that artifact hash to an npm fallback - keeps npm source-profile integrity SRI-only`n- fails closed for install synthesis when hosted candidates are missing explicit `state: available` or `publisher.trust: official``n- validates manifest install `sourceRef` for custom/overridden feed profiles even when top-level candidates are present  ## Stack context  Merged predecessors:  - #95846: feed-shaped bundled catalog fallback - #95868: guarded hosted fetch and hosted metadata preservation - #95877: in-memory last-known-good snapshot fallback contract - #95964: persisted hosted snapshot cache in OpenClaw state  This PR is the next source-profile/trust validation layer. Later marketplace/config PRs must opt into any search/startup/marketplace consumption explicitly.  ## Boundary  Persisted hosted feed bodies remain inert cache material until this and later PRs explicitly validate source profiles and feed trust rules. This PR validates local source profiles and trusted ClawHub install candidates, but does not add broader marketplace refresh/search/startup activation.  ## Validation  Local validation on restacked head `6dc6ca154c7f0000a6961a347a2d572a2493b8a6`:  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` - `CI=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (44 tests) - `pnpm tsgo:core` - `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` - `codex review --base origin/main` on `8bb652b2e5` found the ClawHub-hash/npm-fallback issue; fixed on `4cbec6c7f5` by no longer emitting `npmSpec` for ClawHub-source candidates. A follow-up review on `4cbec6c7f5` found a custom-feed manifest sourceRef bypass; fixed on `453680c88d`. ClawSweeper then flagged the extra `register.openclaw.ai` direct-feed allowlist host; fixed on `6dc6ca154c` by keeping direct overrides constrained to `clawhub.ai`.  ## Real behavior proof  Behavior or issue addressed: Hosted ClawHub feed entries are accepted only through configured local source profiles, and trusted `public-clawhub` candidates normalize live ClawHub `sha256:<hex>` integrity values into SRI install integrity.  Real environment tested: Windows source harness in `C:\src\openclaw-prflow\.worktrees\pr-95969` at head `40fd65584efa35a8363b7d7833677a0b956f5e20`, using the live ClawHub feed at `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins` with no mocked feed body.  Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Ran `node --import tsx -` from the PR worktree. 2. The harness called `loadHostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogEntries({ snapshotStore: null })` and inspected resolved install metadata with `resolveOfficialExternalPluginInstall(entry)`.  Evidence after fix:  ```json {   "url": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins",   "source": "hosted",   "entryCount": 59,   "feed": {     "id": "clawhub-official",     "schemaVersion": 1,     "sequence": 17,     "generatedAt": "2026-06-28T01:24:39.896Z"   },   "metadata": {     "status": 200,     "etag": "\"sha256:ba687b54daeb2ed65e3f963e5be86214bd0421be6d19cbc76595d36374911a5d-gzip\"",     "lastModified": "Sun, 28 Jun 2026 01:24:46 GMT",     "checksum": "sha256:ba687b54daeb2ed65e3f963e5be86214bd0421be6d19cbc76595d36374911a5d"   },   "installableSample": [     {       "id": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw",       "sourceRefs": ["public-clawhub"],       "install": {         "clawhubSpec": "clawhub:@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw@1.0.4",         "npmSpec": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw@1.0.4",         "defaultChoice": "clawhub",         "expectedIntegrity": "sha256-s1XdoEQDvsqri7qwaf0eewV4Ji50WeWYzFsZYVtb2rk="       }     }   ],   "unknownSourceEntryCount": 0 } ```  Observed result after fix: The live hosted feed loaded as `hosted`, returned 59 entries, preserved the `clawhub-official` feed metadata, exposed trusted `public-clawhub` install candidates as ClawHub installs, normalized the live ClawHub checksum to SRI for the ClawHub install path without emitting an npm fallback for that artifact hash, and did not retain entries with unknown source refs.  What was not tested: No end-user marketplace UI or startup activation path was tested; this PR is limited to source-profile validation and catalog install metadata resolution.

target.title: fix(matrix): warn on accumulated token storage roots
target.body:
﻿Closes #76613

## What Problem This Solves

Matrix token rotation can leave multiple populated token-hash storage roots under the same account directory. Current storage-root resolution can still choose the correct active root, but operators get no signal that stale compatible roots have accumulated, which makes crypto-store debugging and cleanup harder.

The previous candidate PR #94644 was closed unmerged after review because it warned for a single legitimate reusable token-rotation sibling and did not expose parseable warning metadata or docs guidance.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change keeps Matrix storage selection semantics unchanged and adds observability only when true multi-root accumulation exists: more than one compatible populated token-hash root is present. The warning includes structured metadata for `parentDir`, `canonicalTokenHash`, `selectedTokenHash`, populated root hashes, sibling hashes, and count.

It also documents the operational distinction between one normal older token-hash root and multiple populated roots that should be inspected and archived carefully.

AI-assisted: prepared with Codex and verified locally.

## User Impact

Matrix operators get a clear warning when stale populated storage roots accumulate after token rotations, without being told to clean up the normal single-root continuity case. The warning is machine-parseable and the docs explain safe manual cleanup.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/storage.test.ts`
- `node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check --threads=1 extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/storage.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/storage.test.ts docs/channels/matrix.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/storage.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/storage.test.ts`
- Redacted emitted-warning diagnostic via `node_modules\.bin\tsx.cmd --input-type=module -`:

```json
{
  "resolvedTokenHash": "348e9df2a42bd6e3",
  "warningCount": 1,
  "warnings": [
    {
      "message": "matrix: multiple populated token-hash storage roots detected",
      "meta": {
        "parentDir": "<stateDir>/matrix/accounts/default/matrix.example.org__bot_example.org",
        "canonicalTokenHash": "348e9df2a42bd6e3",
        "selectedTokenHash": "348e9df2a42bd6e3",
        "populatedTokenHashes": [
          "348e9df2a42bd6e3",
          "9bdf10a691a1cfda"
        ],
        "populatedSiblingTokenHashes": [
          "9bdf10a691a1cfda"
        ],
        "populatedRootCount": 2
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Attempted `pnpm docs:list`, but this Codex checkout triggered a pnpm install/module purge prompt and failed in non-interactive mode with `ERR_PNPM_ABORTED_REMOVE_MODULES_DIR_NO_TTY`; the relevant Matrix docs section was read directly.

target.title: fix: scanned PDF pages reach chat vision models
target.body:
Closes #96541

AI-assisted by Codex.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users sending scanned or image-only PDFs through chat channels would have those rendered PDF page images extracted by media understanding but silently dropped before the reply model saw them.

The affected workflow is chat-channel replies with file attachment extraction enabled, especially scanned PDFs that have no extractable text and need vision input.

## Why This Change Was Made

This threads rendered PDF page images from media-understanding file extraction into the normal reply image pipeline instead of treating them as text-only extraction side effects. It preserves attachment ordering across described media, rendered file pages, and native current-turn images, and applies the same behavior to the ACP dispatch path.

The stale placeholder that said rendered PDF images were not forwarded is removed from runtime output and docs. Local PDF extraction also now honors the existing input-file timeout limit so a stalled extraction path does not hang indefinitely.

## User Impact

Users can attach scanned or image-only PDFs in chat channels and expect vision-capable reply models to receive the rendered pages. The model now has access to the actual PDF page images instead of only seeing a placeholder that the PDF was rendered.

## Evidence

- Focused tests passed: `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.message-hooks.test.ts src/media/input-files.fetch-guard.test.ts src/media/pdf-extract.test.ts extensions/document-extract/document-extractor.test.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts src/media-understanding/media-understanding-misc.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/current-turn-images.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.media-paths.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -- --reporter=dot` passed 5 Vitest shards / 281 tests.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` reported `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed on Blacksmith Testbox `tbx_01kw3ve1hw53mx08yn0t05egst` (`swift-crayfish`), exit 0. This covered the code/test diff before the final docs-only edit.
- Docs validation after the docs edit passed: `pnpm docs:list` and `pnpm docs:check-mdx`.
- Autoreview passed with no accepted/actionable findings.
- Linux source extraction Crabbox proof passed on Azure Crabbox `cbx_38ab3f65a3e0`, run `run_ee855ee7e830`: rendered PDF fallback produced `imageCount=1`, placeholder `[PDF content rendered to images]`, and no stale `images not forwarded` placeholder.
- Live Telegram post-fix proof passed on Azure Crabbox `cbx_19bc9a4ae812`, run `run_fb1b4f306692`: real Telegram `telegram` credential, sent scanned PDF message `25170`, and observed `LIVE_PROOF_PASS issue=96541 transport=telegram credentialKind=telegram imageInputCount=1`.

target.title: refactor: flip sessions and transcripts to sqlite storage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Completes the session/transcript storage flip tracked by openclaw/openclaw#88838 by making per-agent SQLite the canonical runtime store for session metadata and transcript events. Legacy `sessions.json` and active transcript JSONL files are now doctor import inputs or archive artifacts, not runtime fallback state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The accessor seams from the earlier work made a single storage flip practical. Shipping a mixed file/SQLite runtime would let session metadata and transcript rows drift, so this PR moves import, runtime access, retention, SDK cleanup, and ratchets together behind the permanent accessor boundary.

## User Impact

Existing legacy session stores can be migrated non-destructively through doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect flows. Runtime session creation, resume, transcript append, compaction, and maintenance use SQLite rows; retention still exports archived JSONL artifacts before deleting rows so support and archive workflows keep inspectable artifacts.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.conformance.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.session-lifecycle-mutation.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean after two accepted findings were fixed.
- Copied local OpenClaw state proof under `/tmp/openclaw-sqlite-flip-final-20260625T013042Z`: doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect on `codex-proof` and `codex-image-proof` reported zero issues; import wrote 2 entries plus 15 and 18 transcript events respectively.
- Isolated runtime smoke created an agent SQLite DB, created/resumed a session, appended transcript rows, compacted 4 rows to 3, and archive-before-delete cleanup removed 1 lifecycle entry after writing 1 archive artifact.

commands.review_summary:
Adds a root Microsoft ecosystem tracker markdown file, a GitHub CLI generator/PR-body updater script, and a release-owned changelog entry for Microsoft-related issue and PR tracking.

PR surface: Docs +424, Other +428. Total +852 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a user bug report. The PR defects are source-reproducible from the generator and generated body: raw titles are copied into PR-body output, priority/category derivation is heuristic-only, and no package check path exists.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review is needed because the PR is protected, draft, merge-conflicting, missing contributor proof, and still needs maintainer direction on whether this tracker belongs as durable repo automation.

target.title: WORKING: All Microsoft Issues and PRs (refresh)
target.body:
# Microsoft Ecosystem Issues & PRs Tracker

> **Purpose:** Living checklist for maintainers to track all open Microsoft-related issues and PRs (Teams, Windows, WSL, Azure, M365/SharePoint).
>
> **How to use:**
>
> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale
> - Items marked _(stale)_ have been flagged by the stale bot due to inactivity
>
> **Last updated:** 2026-06-27 (post-purge audit: refreshed from currently open GitHub issues/PRs and rebuilt from PR #49126 format)

---

## Summary

| Category | Issues | PRs | Total | Closed | Remaining |
| -------- | ------ | --- | ----- | ------ | --------- |
| MS Teams (channel plugin) | 11 | 47 | 58 | 0 | 58 |
| Windows platform | 51 | 40 | 91 | 0 | 91 |
| WSL | 9 | 1 | 10 | 0 | 10 |
| Azure | 8 | 4 | 12 | 0 | 12 |
| SharePoint / M365 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **79** | **92** | **171** | **0** | **171** |

---

target.title: fix(xai): bound video response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

On the success path, the xAI video generation provider read the create and poll JSON responses with an unbounded `await response.json()`. The response body comes from an external, untrusted endpoint, so a hostile or buggy server that streams a very large body with no `Content-Length` could force the runtime to buffer the entire payload into memory before parsing it — an OOM / hang vector. This mirrors the sibling provider hardening landed in #96604–#96608 and #96875.

## Changes

- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.ts`: `readXaiVideoJson` (used by both the create and poll paths) now reads the body through the shared bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB cap) and parses the resulting bytes, instead of `await response.json()`. On overflow it throws `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and cancels the stream; malformed/non-object payloads keep the existing `xAI video generation response malformed` behavior. The binary video download path already used `readResponseWithLimit` and is unchanged.
- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.test.ts`: existing mocks switched to real streamed `Response` bodies, plus two new regressions that drive an unbounded create/poll body and assert the bounded throw and stream cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The xAI video create and poll control JSON from an untrusted external endpoint must not be buffered whole on the success path. An oversized no-`Content-Length` body must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error. Small valid JSON must continue to parse normally.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` and a would-stream size of ≈64 MiB. The script drove the provider's exact success-path wrapper `readXaiVideoJson` — reproduced byte-for-byte, calling the same exported bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` with the identical 16 MiB cap and overflow error text — over a real loopback fetch + socket, for both the create and poll routes. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx scratchpad/xai-bound-proof.mts` — start the streaming server → call the create-path JSON reader and assert bounded throw + server-side socket abort near the cap → call the poll-path JSON reader and assert the same → run a negative control using old unbounded `fetch().arrayBuffer()` against the same streaming body → call the reader against a small valid JSON response.
- **Observed result after fix**: create JSON threw `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and aborted after 19,464,207 bytes; poll JSON threw the same and aborted after 19,136,527 bytes. The negative control buffered the full 67,108,879 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path parsed `request_id=req_happy status=done`. Focused Vitest: 15 passed.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live xAI service; a live endpoint would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. Because the xAI video provider hard-codes `allowPrivateNetwork: false` and does not expose a per-request SSRF policy, the full exported provider cannot be driven over a loopback socket (the SSRF guard rejects 127.0.0.1); the TCP proof therefore drives the provider's exact success-path JSON wrapper and the real shared bounded reader over a real socket, and the create/poll provider wiring is covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same reader and error text. In practice the control JSON is small; this is defense-in-depth. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

Real `node:http` terminal proof (real loopback socket, exact provider success-path wrapper, real shared bounded reader):

```
[proof] real node:http server on http://127.0.0.1:43519, cap=16777216 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes per overflow route
PASS  create JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  create JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19464207 (<33554432)
PASS  poll JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  poll JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19136527 (<33554432)
PASS  negative control: OLD unbounded read buffers PAST the 16MiB cap :: buffered=67108879 bytes (> 16777216)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded reads :: unbounded buffered=67108879 vs create sent≈19464207
PASS  happy path: small create JSON still parses :: request_id=req_happy status=done

[proof] 7 PASS / 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused Vitest:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

Type checks:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions        # exit 0
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test   # exit 0
```

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix: log pending sync and provider init errors on manager close
target.body:
Closes #96766

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where close-time failures of pending memory sync or provider initialization were silently swallowed, making it impossible to diagnose shutdown-time data-loss or indexing problems.

## Why This Change Was Made

`awaitPendingManagerWork` previously used empty `catch {}` blocks for both `pendingSync` and `pendingProviderInit`, discarding all rejections. The change threads an optional `onError` callback through the helper and wires `MemoryIndexManager.close()` to log those failures via the existing memory subsystem logger, matching the error-reporting pattern already used elsewhere in the memory manager.

## User Impact

Operators and developers will now see warning logs when a memory sync or provider initialization fails during manager close, making shutdown-time data-loss or indexing issues diagnosable instead of silent.

## Evidence

```
$ git diff --check
(no output)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts --configLoader runner extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.async-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts

      Tests  61 passed (61)
   Duration  81.43s (transform 14.38s, setup 1.65s, import 26.37s, tests 52.11s, environment 0ms)
```

target.title: feat(config): support SecretRef for phone numbers in channel configs
target.body:
## Summary

Phone numbers are PII. If you're putting them in `allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, or `groupAllowFrom`, they're sitting in plaintext config. This PR adds SecretRef support for phone number fields across all messaging channels so those values can live in a secret provider instead.

- **Problem:** Phone numbers in channel configs (`allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, `groupAllowFrom`, `account`, `reactionAllowlist`) only accepted plain strings — no way to keep them out of plaintext config files.
- **Why it matters:** Phone numbers are PII under GDPR/CCPA. SecretRef support lets users store them in env vars, files, or exec-based secret providers.
- **What changed:** WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, BlueBubbles, and voice-call channels now accept `SecretRef` objects for all phone number / sender identity fields. Exported TypeScript types, Zod schemas, secret contracts, runtime resolution, config UI hints, docs, and tests are all updated.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing plain string configs continue to work. No breaking changes to the config schema.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #56502
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature. Tests are added for coverage, not regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `config-schema.test.ts` and `secret-contract.test.ts` for each channel, `zod-schema.secretref-allowfrom.test.ts`, `target-registry.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: SecretRef objects are accepted in phone number fields; invalid SecretRef shapes are rejected; `normalizeAllowFrom` filters out unresolved SecretRef objects instead of stringifying them to `[object Object]`; secret target registry discovers plugin manifest `secretInputs.paths`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Schema-level tests catch accept/reject at the boundary; the `normalizeAllowFrom` test catches the runtime filtering; the target registry test catches manifest-driven secret discovery
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A — new feature surface
- If no new test is added, why not: Tests are added

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `allowFrom`, `defaultTo`, `groupAllowFrom` (and Signal's `account`, `reactionAllowlist`) now accept SecretRef objects in addition to plain strings across WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles.
- Voice-call's `fromNumber`, `toNumber`, and `allowFrom` accept SecretRef objects.
- Config UI hints mark these fields as `sensitive: true` so UIs mask them.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes — phone number fields can now be stored as SecretRefs
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: SecretRefs are resolved through the existing secret provider pipeline. No new secret resolution paths. `normalizeAllowFrom` filters out unresolved SecretRef objects so they can't leak as `[object Object]` strings into allowlists.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Devin VM)
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, BlueBubbles, voice-call
- Relevant config (redacted): `"allowFrom": [{ "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "MY_PHONE" }]`

### Steps

1. Add a SecretRef to a phone number field in config (e.g. `"allowFrom": [{ "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "MY_PHONE" }]`)
2. Run `openclaw doctor` or start the gateway
3. The SecretRef is resolved through the configured secret provider

### Expected

- Config parses successfully with SecretRef objects in phone number fields
- Plain strings continue to work as before

### Actual

- Confirmed via unit tests (73 tests across 8 test files, all passing)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

All tests pass: `pnpm tsgo` (typecheck), `pnpm check` (lint), `pnpm test` (73 relevant tests across schema validation, secret contracts, runtime normalization, and target registry).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Schema acceptance/rejection for all 5 channels, `normalizeAllowFrom` SecretRef filtering, secret target registry manifest discovery, voice-call manifest path merging after rebase
- Edge cases checked: Invalid SecretRef shapes rejected, mixed arrays of strings + SecretRefs + numbers handled correctly, undefined input returns empty array, objects with custom `toString()` still work in `normalizeStringEntries`
- What I did **not** verify: End-to-end with a running gateway and real secret provider

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No (additive only — existing configs unchanged)
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unresolved SecretRef objects could reach `normalizeAllowFrom` at runtime
  - Mitigation: `normalizeAllowFrom` now filters out SecretRef objects via `isSecretRef()` before string normalization, preventing `[object Object]` entries in allowlists

---

GPT-5 (OpenAI Codex) via Codex
Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic) via Devin

target.title: feat(tasks): improve task state transitions and lifecycle formatting
target.body:
## Summary
- add explicit intermediate active task states for approval waits and external waits
- propagate those active-state semantics across registry maintenance, flow guards, CLI views, and auto-reply task rendering
- add compressed operational summaries plus shared task lifecycle event formatting for state-change updates
- reuse the shared lifecycle formatter in task update delivery paths and extend focused task coverage

## Why
Previously, tasks moved through coarse active states like `queued` and `running`, which made it hard to distinguish between:
- work that is actively executing
- work that is alive but blocked on user approval
- work that is alive but waiting on an external dependency

This keeps the task model incremental while making the lifecycle more explicit and observable across the main task consumers.

## Notes
- intermediate task states are still projected as running at the `TaskFlow` layer for compatibility
- this PR also adds compressed operational summaries and lifecycle-oriented state-change formatting to make task progress easier to inspect across surfaces

## Testing
- OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:fast -- src/tasks/task-status.test.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts
- OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:fast -- src/tasks/task-status.test.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.store.test.ts
- existing focused task-state tests retained in the PR history for intermediate active state semantics

target.title: fix: allow custom control UI origins
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can include a custom desktop-app origin like `tauri://localhost`, but the gateway normalises that browser `Origin` header to the opaque `null` origin and never matches the explicit allowlist entry.
- Why it matters: Tauri/Electron-style Control UI wrappers have to fall back to wildcard origins even when operators want a specific origin allowlist.
- What changed: preserve the exact `scheme://host` origin for non-special URL schemes when the browser sends a scheme and host, then continue matching through the existing lower-case allowlist path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): literal `Origin: null` remains rejected, unlisted custom origins remain rejected, and host-header/local-loopback fallbacks keep their existing rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #46520
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` returns the opaque string `null`, so exact allowlist matching compares `tauri://localhost` with `null`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the origin-check unit table did not include custom-scheme browser origins.
- Contributing context (if known): browser desktop wrappers can send non-special scheme origins even though those schemes do not have a standard web origin serialisation.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: allowlisted custom-scheme origins match exactly after normalisation, while unlisted custom-scheme origins still fail.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to origin normalisation before the existing allowlist check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators can set `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` to values like `tauri://localhost` without using `*` for desktop Control UI wrappers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
tauri://localhost -> URL.origin "null" -> allowlist miss

After:
tauri://localhost -> preserved custom origin -> explicit allowlist match
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. This keeps explicit allowlist matching required for non-wildcard custom-scheme origins.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway Control UI origin check
- Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins = ["tauri://localhost"]`

### Steps

1. Configure `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` with `tauri://localhost`.
2. Connect with browser `Origin: tauri://localhost`.
3. Compare against `checkBrowserOrigin` behaviour.

### Expected

- The explicit custom-scheme allowlist entry is accepted.
- Unlisted custom-scheme origins are rejected.

### Actual

- Current main sees `new URL("tauri://localhost").origin` as `null`, so the explicit allowlist entry does not match.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local command evidence:

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed.
- Manual Node parser check passed for `tauri://LOCALHOST`, `electron://localhost`, `https://CONTROL.example.com`, literal `null`, and malformed `not a url` cases.
- `pnpm docs:list` was attempted but blocked by Corepack registry timeout while downloading `pnpm 10.33.0` (`UND_ERR_CONNECT_TIMEOUT`).
- `pnpm test src/gateway/origin-check.test.ts` reached the repo test wrapper via cached pnpm but could not run because this worktree has no `vitest` dependency and dependency installation is blocked by registry timeouts.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: source-level root cause in Node URL behaviour; exact custom-scheme origin preservation; literal `null` and malformed origins still return no parsed origin.
- Edge cases checked: upper-case custom origin host normalises to lower case; unlisted custom scheme remains rejected by the unit table.

target.title: fix(feishu): allow number/boolean/array types in bitable fields parameter
target.body:
## Problem

The `fields` parameter in `CreateRecordSchema` and `UpdateRecordSchema` uses `Type.Any()` which serializes to an empty JSON Schema `{}`. This makes the LLM default to passing all values as **strings**, even for numeric fields where `number` type is expected.

For example, the LLM passes `{"Price": "99"}` instead of `{"Price": 99}`, causing type mismatches in the Bitable API.

## Fix

Change `Type.Any()` to `Type.Union([Type.String(), Type.Number(), Type.Boolean(), Type.Array(Type.Any()), Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Any())])` so the JSON Schema explicitly advertises `"type": ["string", "number", "boolean", "array", "object"]`.

### Changes
- **`CreateRecordSchema.fields`** (line 533): `Type.Any()` → explicit union type
- **`UpdateRecordSchema.fields`** (line 575): same change

Both `property` in `CreateFieldSchema` keeps `Type.Any()` as-is, since field creation properties have field-type-specific structures that are too varied to constrain.

target.title: fix(moonshot): prevent reasoning_content leak when reasoning is disabled
target.body:
## Summary
- When a model like kimi-k2.6 returns `reasoning_content` in stream deltas even with reasoning disabled, the parser still detected the field and marked strict content partitioning, causing reasoning text to leak into visible chat output
- Fix: only scan for reasoning fields (`reasoning_content`, `reasoning`, `reasoning_text`) when `shouldEmitReasoning` is true, so disabled-reasoning models don't trigger content partitioning from unprocessed reasoning deltas

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify moonshot/kimi-k2.6 with reasoning OFF no longer shows internal reasoning text in chat output
- [ ] Verify moonshot/kimi-k2.6 with reasoning ON still correctly emits thinking blocks
- [ ] Verify other OpenAI-compatible providers (DeepSeek, llama.cpp) still work correctly

Closes #96732

target.title: fix(mcp): allow SecretRef values in mcp.servers env and headers [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds SecretRef support to core MCP server configuration so `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` can use SecretInput values in addition to plaintext literals.

Before this change, core `mcp.servers` only accepted primitive literal values on these surfaces, which meant MCP credentials on the built-in config path had to remain plaintext even though other SecretRef-capable surfaces already supported resolved runtime credentials.

## What changed

- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.env.*`
- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.headers.*`
- include core MCP env/header targets in command/runtime secret resolution
- resolve only the active MCP transport surface at runtime
- include core MCP env/header targets in the supported SecretRef target registry
- teach `secrets configure` and `secrets audit` to handle MCP env/header targets without promoting generic non-secret literals like URLs and feature flags
- update the MCP and SecretRef docs/matrix to document the supported surface

## Why

This keeps sensitive MCP credentials out of plaintext config while preserving the existing runtime model:

- config stores SecretRefs
- activation resolves active refs into the in-memory runtime snapshot
- spawned MCP processes receive resolved string values at launch time

That is consistent with the rest of OpenClaw’s SecretRef model and removes a real gap on the core `mcp.servers` surface.

## Validation

Passed locally:

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`

Passed targeted coverage for the touched surface:

- `src/config/config-misc.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/configure-plan.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-core-snapshots.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-inactive-core-surfaces.test.ts`
- `src/commands/agent.runtime-config.test.ts`
- `src/cli/command-secret-targets.test.ts`
- `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- targeted `src/secrets/audit.test.ts` coverage for MCP plaintext detection heuristics

Additional functional validation:

- verified a core stdio MCP server configured through `mcp.servers.*.env.*` receives resolved string env values at launch time while the config itself still stores SecretRef objects
- verified custom credential-like names such as `SERVICE_KEY` and `X-Access-Key` are treated as sensitive by configure/audit flows
- verified generic literals such as URLs and feature-flag headers are not promoted as secret targets

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` only accepted primitive string literals before this PR. Passing a SecretRef object (`{"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}`) as an MCP env or header value caused `config validate` to return `Invalid input`. This PR widens the accepted surface so both `env` and `headers` fields on core MCP servers store SecretInput values, with runtime resolution delivering the resolved string to the spawned MCP process.

- Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04 (HAL server), OpenClaw built from PR head `24e3416d75b` (runtime captured at parent `9598ab1` — `24e3416d75b` changes test-file module paths only, no runtime delta). 1Password CLI service account for secret backing.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Write `openclaw.json` with `mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY = {"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}` and `mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization = {"source":"env","id":"MC_AUTH_TOKEN"}`
  2. `openclaw config validate`
  3. `openclaw secrets audit` (env vars absent — SecretRefs unresolved)
  4. `MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit`

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output, values redacted):

  ```
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (9598ab1)

  $ openclaw config validate
  Config valid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json

  $ openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: unresolved. plaintext=0, unresolved=3, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_API_KEY (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_AUTH_TOKEN (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:models.providers.anthropic.apiKey
      Failed to resolve env:default:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (variable missing)

  $ MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: clean. plaintext=0, unresolved=0, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  ```

- Observed result after fix: Config validates cleanly with SecretRef objects in both `env.*` and `headers.*`. Secrets audit identifies `MC_API_KEY` and `Authorization` as unresolved SecretRefs and correctly ignores `MC_URL` (URL-shaped) and `X-Feature-Flag` (generic literal). With env vars set, audit is fully clean (`plaintext=0, unresolved=0`).

- What was not tested: Live end-to-end MCP spawn with SecretRef values resolved from exec provider (HAL `/tmp` is mounted `noexec`, blocking shell-script exec tests locally). MCP session runtime SecretRef injection is covered by unit tests in `pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `/tmp` is mounted `noexec` on the test machine, preventing exec-provider shell-script invocation locally. Same exec tests pass in CI (GitHub Actions standard tmpfs is executable). Version shown is `9598ab1` (parent of `24e3416d75b`) — both commits produce identical runtime behavior; `24e3416d75b` only renames test-file module paths.

- Before evidence: Without this PR, `openclaw config validate` on the same config returned:
  ```
  OpenClaw config is invalid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY: Invalid input
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization: Invalid input
  ```

## Scope

This PR is limited to core MCP SecretRef support for `mcp.servers` env/header surfaces and the related docs/tests needed to support that behavior.

It does not include unrelated runtime, deployment, or integration changes.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted.

Testing performed locally with repo checks, targeted tests, and direct runtime validation.
